annotations.label: Curry–Howard correspondence (overview)

anchor.snippet: a direct relationship between computer programs and mathematical proofs

annotations.note: Mathlib is a library of formalized mathematics built on Lean's dependent type theory, which itself embodies Curry–Howard, but the correspondence as a meta-theorem is not stated as a Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Curry's 1934 observation (combinator types as axiom-schemes)

anchor.snippet: the types of the combinators could be seen as axiom-schemes for intuitionistic implicational logic

annotations.note: Mathlib has no formalization of combinatory logic or Hilbert-style axiom schemes, so this historical/meta observation is absent.

annotations.label: Curry's 1958 observation (Hilbert-style deduction ~ combinatory logic)

anchor.snippet: coincides on some fragment with the typed fragment of a standard model of computation known as combinatory logic

annotations.note: Grepping Mathlib for combinatory logic yields nothing; this meta-mathematical coincidence result is not formalized.

annotations.label: Howard's 1969 observation (natural deduction ~ lambda calculus)

anchor.snippet: can be directly interpreted in its intuitionistic version as a typed variant of the model of computation known as lambda calculus

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize natural deduction or lambda calculus as syntactic objects, so this correspondence is not present as a decl.

annotations.label: Curry–Howard slogan: proofs as programs, formulas as types

anchor.snippet: a proof is a program, and the formula it proves is the type for the program

annotations.note: This is a meta-mathematical slogan describing Lean's own foundations, not a Mathlib theorem or definition.

annotations.label: Formulas-as-types correspondence table

anchor.snippet: implication behaves the same as a function type

annotations.note: The formulas-as-types table is a meta-level dictionary about Lean's type theory itself, not a Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Curry's correspondence for axioms K and S

anchor.section: Intuitionistic Hilbert-style deduction systems and typed combinatory logic
anchor.snippet: the simplest types for the basic combinators K and S of combinatory logic surprisingly corresponded to the respective axiom schemes

annotations.note: No K/S combinator or typed combinatory logic formalization exists in Mathlib (grep for "combinatory logic" returns no files).

annotations.label: Hilbert-style derivability (Γ ⊢ δ)

anchor.section: Intuitionistic Hilbert-style deduction systems and typed combinatory logic
anchor.snippet: Let Γ be a finite collection of formulas, considered as hypotheses

annotations.note: Mathlib has no formalized Hilbert-style propositional derivability relation for this presentation of intuitionistic logic.

annotations.label: Typed combinatory logic typing judgment

anchor.section: Intuitionistic Hilbert-style deduction systems and typed combinatory logic
anchor.snippet: let Γ be a finite collection of variables, annotated with their types

annotations.note: Typed combinatory logic as a syntactic typing judgment is not present anywhere in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Peirce's law excluded from intuitionistic correspondence

anchor.section: Intuitionistic Hilbert-style deduction systems and typed combinatory logic
anchor.snippet: some classical tautologies , such as Peirce's law

annotations.note: Mathlib's `peirce` states ((a→b)→a)→a using classical axioms, matching the formula, but the meta-fact that it is *not* derivable in intuitionistic Hilbert systems is not formalized.

annotations.label: No combinator typable with Peirce's law type

anchor.section: Intuitionistic Hilbert-style deduction systems and typed combinatory logic
anchor.snippet: there is no typed term of combinatory logic that is typable with type

annotations.note: This non-existence result about typed combinatory logic terms has no counterpart since combinatory logic is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Combinator X as one-point basis

anchor.section: Intuitionistic Hilbert-style deduction systems and typed combinatory logic
anchor.snippet: the fact that the combinator X constitutes a one-point basis of (extensional) combinatory logic

annotations.note: Combinatory logic bases (like the single combinator X) are not part of Mathlib's scope.

annotations.label: Howard's correspondence (natural deduction ~ simply typed lambda calculus)

anchor.section: Intuitionistic natural deduction and typed lambda calculus
anchor.snippet: a syntactic analogy between the programs of simply typed lambda calculus and the proofs of natural deduction

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes mathematics inside Lean's type theory but does not itself formalize simply typed lambda calculus or natural deduction as separate syntactic systems to state this analogy as a theorem.

annotations.label: Normal forms correspond to normal deductions

anchor.section: Intuitionistic natural deduction and typed lambda calculus
anchor.snippet: the notion of normal forms in lambda calculus matches Prawitz 's notion of normal deduction in natural deduction

annotations.note: No lambda-calculus normal form or natural-deduction normalization theory is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Griffin's extension of Curry–Howard to classical logic

anchor.snippet: The extension of the correspondence to Peirce's law and hence to classical logic became clear from the work of Griffin

annotations.note: Griffin's call/cc-typed-as-Peirce's-law result is a proof-theoretic/PL-theoretic fact outside Mathlib's scope.

annotations.label: Finer classical correspondence (multiple conclusions)

anchor.snippet: A finer Curry–Howard correspondence exists for classical logic if one defines classical logic not by adding an axiom such as Peirce's law

annotations.note: Multiple-conclusion sequent-style classical Curry–Howard is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Proofs-as-programs correspondence for sequent calculus

anchor.snippet: A proofs-as-programs correspondence can be settled for the formalism known as Gentzen 's sequent calculus

annotations.note: Gentzen's sequent calculus is not formalized anywhere in Mathlib (grep for "sequent calculus"/"Gentzen" is empty).

annotations.label: Prawitz's homomorphism (natural deduction to construction terms)

anchor.snippet: Dag Prawitz defined a homomorphism between natural deduction derivations for minimal and intuitionistic first-order logic and construction terms

annotations.note: Prawitz's homomorphism between natural-deduction derivations and construction terms is a historical proof-theory result not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: de Bruijn's independent proofs-as-lambda-terms correspondence

anchor.snippet: used the lambda notation for representing proofs of the theorem checker Automath

annotations.note: This is a historical claim about the Automath system, not a mathematical statement Mathlib would formalize.

annotations.label: BHK interpretation

anchor.snippet: The BHK interpretation interprets intuitionistic proofs as functions

annotations.note: Mathlib is built on classical logic and does not formalize the Brouwer–Heyting–Kolmogorov interpretation of intuitionistic proofs.

annotations.label: Kleene's recursive realizability

anchor.snippet: splits proofs of intuitionistic arithmetic into the pair of a recursive function and of a proof of a formula

annotations.note: No realizability interpretation of intuitionistic arithmetic exists in Mathlib (grep for "realizability" is empty).

annotations.label: Kreisel's modified realizability

anchor.snippet: applies to intuitionistic higher-order predicate logic and shows that the simply typed lambda term inductively extracted from the proof realizes the initial formula

annotations.note: Kreisel's modified realizability is absent from Mathlib, which has no proof-term extraction machinery for intuitionistic higher-order logic.

annotations.label: Gödel's dialectica interpretation

anchor.snippet: dialectica interpretation realizes (an extension of) intuitionistic arithmetic with computable functions

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes de Paiva's categorical Dialectica category `Dial` (a categorification inspired by the interpretation) but not Gödel's original proof-theoretic functional interpretation of intuitionistic arithmetic.

annotations.label: Lambek's theorem (CCC equational theory)

anchor.snippet: the proofs of intuitionistic propositional logic and the combinators of typed combinatory logic share a common equational theory

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes the cartesian-closed-category side (`MonoidalClosed`/`CartesianMonoidalCategory`) but not typed combinatory logic or propositional-proof syntax, so Lambek's equational-theory-coincidence theorem itself is not stated.

annotations.label: Cartesian closed category objects (propositions/types)

anchor.snippet: Objects (propositions/types) include

annotations.note: A cartesian closed category's objects (terminal object, products) are exactly given by `CartesianMonoidalCategory` combined with `MonoidalClosed` for exponentials.

annotations.label: Cartesian closed category morphisms (deductions/terms)

anchor.snippet: Morphisms (deductions/terms) include

annotations.note: `MonoidalClosed.curry`/`uncurry` give the Hom(X⊗Y,Z) ≅ Hom(X, Y⟹Z) adjunction that models CCC morphisms (deduction/term formation), generalized to arbitrary monoidal closed categories.

annotations.label: Curry–Howard–Lambek iff (term exists iff provable)

anchor.snippet: Now, there exists t such that

annotations.note: The CCC-morphism side (Hom-set nonemptiness via curry/uncurry) is formalized, but the logical "provable" side of the biconditional has no counterpart since propositional provability isn't formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Identity combinator as proof of α → α

anchor.section: The identity combinator seen as a proof of α → α in Hilbert-style logic
anchor.snippet: As an example, consider a proof of the theorem

annotations.note: This worked Hilbert-style-derivation example is expository and has no Hilbert-system decl in Mathlib to match against.

annotations.label: Composition combinator (B) as proof of (β→α)→(γ→β)→γ→α

anchor.section: The composition combinator seen as a proof of ( β → α ) → ( γ → β ) → γ → α in Hilbert-style logic
anchor.snippet: let's look at the theorem that corresponds to the B function

annotations.note: This is a worked combinatory-logic example, and combinatory logic is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Natural deduction proof of (β→α)→(γ→β)→γ→α as a λ-term

anchor.section: The normal proof of ( β → α ) → ( γ → β ) → γ → α in natural deduction seen as a λ -term
anchor.snippet: The diagram below gives proof of

annotations.note: This natural-deduction/lambda-term worked example has no formalized natural-deduction system in Mathlib to correspond to.

annotations.label: CCCs generalized by closed monoidal categories

anchor.snippet: cartesian closed categories are generalized by closed monoidal categories

annotations.note: Mathlib literally structures cartesian closed categories as `CartesianMonoidalCategory` plus `MonoidalClosed`, with `MonoidalClosed` alone giving the more general closed monoidal category notion, mirroring this exact generalization.

annotations.label: Correspondence to cobordisms

anchor.snippet: these can be shown to correspond to cobordisms

annotations.note: Mathlib has no cobordism category or TQFT formalization (grep for "cobordism" returns no files).

annotations.label: Univalence axiom

anchor.snippet: type theory is extended by the univalence axiom

annotations.note: Mathlib is built on classical Lean foundations (propext, choice) and does not adopt or formalize the univalence axiom (grep for "univalence" is empty).

annotations.label: Homotopy type theory generalization (proofs as paths)

anchor.snippet: the Curry–Howard correspondence that proofs are elements of inhabited types is generalized to the notion of homotopic equivalence of proofs

annotations.note: Mathlib does not implement homotopy type theory (no univalence, no proofs-as-paths machinery), so this generalization is absent.
